desc: The most commonly encountered sphinx moth. Hovers at flowers like a hummingbird at dusk. Caterpillar is the familiar "hornworm" of gardens.

fm.Upperside: Olive-brown FW with white diagonal stripe; HW pink with black bands
fm.Behavior: Hovers at flowers at dusk; strong swift flight
fm.Flight: Mar–Nov; multiple broods; mass migrations some years
fm.Hosts: Extremely polyphagous — evening primrose, portulaca, four o'clock, grape
fm.Habitat: Gardens, desert, roadsides — any habitat during migration

hp: Many families inc. Onagraceae (Oenothera), Vitaceae (Vitis), Portulacaceae. Visited by/prey for hummingbirds.

desc: Carolina sphinx — huge — one of the largest moths in LA. Larva is the familiar tobacco/tomato hornworm of gardens, with diagonal white stripes and posterior horn. Widely used in scientific research — one of the most studied insects for insect physiology and neurobiology.

fm.Upperside: Gray-brown with wavy dark lines; 6 orange lateral abdominal spots (vs. 5 in M. quinquemaculatus)
fm.Behavior: Strong nocturnal flight; visits tubular flowers

hp: Solanaceae: tobacco, tomato, pepper, Datura

desc: Gray sphinx moth with wavy dark lines creating an elegant pattern. Associated with chaparral species. One of the more commonly seen sphingids.

fm.Upperside: Smooth gray with fine dark streaks
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; attracted to lights

hp: Lamiaceae: Monardella, Salvia; also Eriodictyon

desc: Large sphinx moth in pink and brown. Larvae (the 'grape hornworm') feed on grape vines and Virginia creeper. Spectacular pink hindwings.

fm.Upperside: FW brown with dark patches; HW pink
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; rapid flight

hp: Vitaceae: grape (Vitis), Virginia creeper (Parthenocissus)

desc: Day-flying moth that mimics a bumblebee with clear wings. Hovers at flowers like a hummingbird. Larvae on snowberry and honeysuckle.

fm.Upperside: Clear wings with dark borders; bumblebee-like fuzzy body
fm.Behavior: Diurnal — hovers like hummingbird at flowers

hp: Caprifoliaceae: Lonicera (honeysuckle), Symphoricarpos (snowberry). Visited by/prey for hummingbirds.

desc: Day-flying moth with clear wings and olive body. Hovers at flowers like a hummingbird. Distinguished from Snowberry by reddish-brown thorax. Rare in LA.

fm.Upperside: Clear wings; red-brown thorax; olive abdomen
fm.Behavior: Diurnal hummingbird mimic
fm.Habitat: Woodland edges; rare in LA County

hp: Important pollinators — hovering flight with long proboscis. Hornworm larvae on specific hosts. On olive.

desc: Giant silk moth with spectacular hindwing eyespots — large transparent 'windows' ringed in yellow, blue, and black. Named for the one-eyed Cyclops Polyphemus. Non-feeding adult lives only a few days. Larvae on many trees. Wingspan to 6 inches.

fm.Upperside: Tan-brown with large blue-purple-yellow eyespots on HW; transparent FW window
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; attracted to lights; adults do not feed (no functional mouthparts)
fm.Habitat: Oak woodland, riparian, urban with host trees

hp: Giant silk moths. Larvae on trees. Adults non-feeding. Stinging caterpillars in some species. Ant associate.

desc: Large reddish-brown saturniid with crescent-shaped wing marks. Western replacement for the famous eastern Cecropia moth. Larvae on ceanothus, willow, and many other plants. Impressive wingspan to 5 inches. Adults do not feed — live only days.

fm.Upperside: Reddish-brown with white crescent marks and red-white marginal bands
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; adults non-feeding; ♂ detects ♀ pheromone miles away

hp: Ceanothus silk moth — large saturniid. Larvae on Ceanothus, Prunus, Arctostaphylos, and other native shrubs. Cocoon attached to twig.

desc: One of the most stunning moths in LA County. Cream forewings with intricate network of black lines above; hindwings vivid red-orange with black spots. Day-flying. The combination of elaborate line-work and red hindwings is unmistakable.

fm.Upperside: FW cream with bold black network lines; HW red-orange with black spots
fm.Behavior: Diurnal slow flier; conspicuous; warning coloration

hp: Fabaceae: Lupinus (lupines), Astragalus

desc: Day-flying tiger moth of mountain chaparral. Highly polymorphic — dark FW with white spots; HW orange/red with black. Multiple color forms. Transverse Ranges populations.

fm.Upperside: Dark brown FW with cream spots; HW orange with dark spots
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; warning coloration

hp: Day-flying tiger moth of mountain chaparral. Highly polymorphic — multiple color forms. Larvae feed on low-growing plants in montane scrub.

desc: Adult is plain orange-buff. Famous for the 'woolly bear' caterpillar — the banded brown-and-black larvae crossing roads in fall, subject of folklore claiming band width predicts winter severity (it doesn't — band width varies with age and food quality).

fm.Upperside: Plain orange-buff with small dark spots
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; attracted to lights

hp: Polyphagous: plantain, dandelion, nettle, clover, many herbs

desc: Large saturniid with dramatic hindwing eyespots. When threatened, forewings part to reveal huge blue-and-yellow eyespots — a startling anti-predator display. Males bright yellow; females red-brown. STINGING CATERPILLAR — green with urticating rosette spines causing intense pain.

fm.Upperside: ♂: yellow FW, huge blue-black eyespot on yellow HW. ♀: red-brown with same eyespot.
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; flashes eyespots when disturbed; adults non-feeding; caterpillar stings

hp: Io moth — dramatic hindwing eyespots startle predators. STINGING caterpillar. Polyphagous larvae on many trees and shrubs. Painful urticating spines.

desc: Spotted yucca moth — tiny white moth in one of nature's most famous obligate mutualisms. The ONLY pollinator of yucca flowers — female actively collects pollen, stuffs it into stigma, then lays eggs in ovary. Larvae eat some seeds; plant sacrifices some seeds for pollination.

fm.Upperside: Pure white; narrow wings
fm.Behavior: Deliberately pollinates yucca while ovipositing; obligate mutualism

hp: Obligate yucca pollinator mutualism (Pellmyr 2003, Ann Missouri Bot Garden). Only pollinator of Yucca whipplei. Co-evolved for millions of years.

desc: Cabbage looper — mottled gray-brown noctuid with diagnostic silvery figure-8 (ni) mark on forewings. One of the most common and destructive garden pests — semi-looper larvae devastate brassicas, lettuce, and many vegetables. The silver mark is unique and diagnostic.

fm.Upperside: Mottled gray-brown; silver figure-8 on FW
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; strong flier; attracted to lights

hp: Brassicaceae, Fabaceae, Solanaceae — highly polyphagous crop pest

desc: Introduced from SE Asia (first NA record Oregon, 1876). Gray-brown forewings with pale bean-shaped spot. Larvae devastate vegetables, cotton, and ornamentals — a major California agricultural pest. Overwinters in SoCal. Highly resistant to conventional insecticides. Larvae gregarious early, solitary later. Up to five generations per year in LA.

fm.Upperside: Gray-brown with small pale kidney-shaped mark
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; mass flights to lights
fm.Hosts: Extremely polyphagous — beets, lettuce, cotton, ornamentals

hp: Highly polyphagous: beet, alfalfa, cotton, corn, many vegetables

desc: Large yellow underwing — introduced European noctuid now abundant across NA. Plain brown forewings conceal bright orange-yellow hindwings with black border — a flash-and-hide startle display. Very common at lights. The hindwing flash is dramatic and unexpected.

fm.Upperside: Brown FW; orange-yellow HW with dark border
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; hides in soil by day; strong flier

hp: Introduced European noctuid — now one of the most abundant moths in NA. Larvae (cutworms) feed on diverse garden plants. Bright orange hindwings diagnostic.

desc: Camouflaged looper moth — tiny bright green 'emerald' geometrid. Larvae attach flower petals and plant fragments to their bodies as remarkable camouflage.

fm.Upperside: Bright emerald green with faint white lines
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; caterpillar decorates itself with flower parts for camo

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Ant associate. Geometridae.

desc: Extreme sexual dimorphism: males brown and winged; females completely wingless, laying eggs directly on their cocoon. Caterpillars spectacular — colorful with dense tufts (tussocks) of hair. Periodic defoliating outbreaks on oaks and ornamentals.

fm.Upperside: ♂: gray-brown with faint darker bands. ♀: wingless, plump.
fm.Behavior: ♂ flies to lights; ♀ never flies; eggs in mass on cocoon; caterpillar showy

hp: Western tussock moth — extreme sexual dimorphism: winged males, flightless females. Polyphagous caterpillars defoliate many native/ornamental trees.

desc: Wild cherry sphinx — large gray sphinx with olive-tinged body. Associated with stone fruits (cherry, plum), olives, and privets. Impressive size.

fm.Upperside: Gray with dark streaks and pale zigzag lines
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; powerful flight; hovers at flowers

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Sphingidae.

desc: Large spectacular sphinx moth in green, pink, and brown. One of the most beautiful moths in LA County. Larvae on grape and Virginia creeper.

fm.Upperside: Olive-green with dark patches and pink highlights
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; tubular flowers

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Desert Wild Grape.

desc: Elegant sheep moth — spectacular day-flying silk moth with bold black-and-yellow or black-and-pink banding. STINGING CATERPILLAR — dense urticating spines cause painful dermatitis lasting days. On Ceanothus and Ribes. One of LA's most beautiful moths.

fm.Upperside: Variable: yellow/black or rose-pink/black banded pattern; HW eyespots
fm.Behavior: Diurnal — unusual for Saturniidae; fast buzzy flight; caterpillar stings

hp: Rosaceae (Rosa), Rhamnaceae (Ceanothus), Salicaceae (Salix)

desc: 'the worm in the apple.' Small gray tortricid whose larva is the most familiar fruit pest worldwide. Bores into apple, pear, and walnut, leaving frass-filled tunnels. One of the world's most economically significant insect pests. Controlled by mating disruption in orchards.

fm.Upperside: Gray with copper-bronze patch at FW tip
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; ♀ lays eggs on developing fruit; larva bores into apple/pear

hp: Rosaceae: apple (Malus), pear (Pyrus), walnut (Juglans)

desc: Small, metallic blue-black day-flying moth. Larvae gregarious on grape leaves, lined up side-by-side as they systematically skeletonize foliage to veins. Important grape pest in California. The metallic blue and diurnal habit are distinctive.

fm.Upperside: Metallic blue-black; orange collar
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; slow buzzy flight; mildly toxic (cyanogenic)

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Desert Wild Grape. Ant associate.

desc: Spectacular large green silk moth. Historically documented in SoCal but now extirpated from the region. Included for ecological/historical context.

fm.Upperside: Pale green with long HW tails; eyespots on each wing
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; adults non-feeding; dramatic long tails

hp: Giant silk moths. Larvae on trees. Adults non-feeding. Stinging caterpillars in some species. Saturniidae.

desc: Tobacco budworm — olive-tan noctuid whose green larvae are a major pest of ornamental flowers (petunias, geraniums, nicotiana) and tobacco. Larvae bore into flower buds, destroying blooms from inside. Notorious for developing insecticide resistance. Economically significant.

fm.Upperside: Olive-tan with pale bands
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; to lights; larvae bore into flower buds

hp: Wood/stem borer. Noctuidae.

desc: Periodic defoliator of mountain conifers. Males gray and winged; females wingless and flightless. Caterpillars showy with red, orange, and white tufts. Outbreaks can defoliate thousands of acres of conifer forest. Important forest pest with cyclic population dynamics.

fm.Upperside: ♂: gray-brown. ♀: wingless, plump.
fm.Behavior: ♂ flies; ♀ never flies; eggs on cocoon; outbreak species

hp: Conifers: Douglas-fir, true firs, spruce

desc: Electra silk moth — day-flying saturniid with bold black-and-white banding. STINGING CATERPILLAR — rosette spines cause severe dermatitis. On buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in coastal sage scrub. SoCal near-endemic. One of the most beautiful native moths in the region.

fm.Upperside: Black with white median band and red-orange HW base
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; fast buzzy flight; caterpillar stings painfully

hp: Electra silk moth — day-flying saturniid. STINGING caterpillar with urticating spines. On native chaparral shrubs. LA County foothill specialist.

desc: White-striped black — day-flying black moth with bold white median stripe across forewing. Distinctive and easily identified — no similar species in LA. Larvae on clematis (Clematis). The bold pattern and diurnal habit make this one of the most recognizable micro-moths.

fm.Upperside: Black with bold white transverse stripe
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; rests with wings spread flat; distinctive pattern

hp: Larvae on clematis (clematis).

desc: Invasive from South America (via biocontrol gone wrong). Threatens native Opuntia. Larvae bore into cactus pads. Advancing westward.

fm.Upperside: Gray-brown; cryptic
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; larvae bore inside Opuntia pads, hollowing them out

hp: Invasive introduction — ecological impact on native communities. Associated with Cactus.

desc: Common household pest. Larvae spin silken tubes in flour, grain, and cereal products. Found in pantries and food storage areas worldwide.

fm.Upperside: FW with dark and pale banded pattern; olive and brown
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; found in kitchens, pantries, grain storage
fm.Hosts: Stored grain, flour, cereals, dried food

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Pyralidae.

desc: Day-flying, jet-black moth with eight cream-white spots and striking orange leg tufts. Larvae on grape and Virginia creeper. The combination of black body, white spots, and orange tufts is unmistakable. One of the most striking day-flying moths.

fm.Upperside: Black with 2 cream-yellow FW spots + 2 HW spots = 8 total
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; visits flowers; distinctive pattern; orange leg tufts

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Desert Wild Grape. Forest habitat.

desc: Hornet clearwing — remarkable hornet mimic with transparent wings, yellow-banded body, and narrow waist. Larvae bore in trunks of poplar and willow for 2-3 years. The mimicry is so convincing that even experienced entomologists do a double-take. Day-flying.

fm.Upperside: Clear wings; yellow and dark brown banded body — hornet mimic
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; convincing hornet mimicry; wasp-like buzzy flight

hp: Larvae on Salix (willows) in riparian habitat. Mimicry specialist.

desc: Sweet potato hornworm moth — huge sphinx moth with distinctive pink spots along abdomen (cingulata = banded). Tropical migrant reaching SoCal in warm years. Larvae on sweet potato and morning glory. One of the largest moths that reaches LA — wingspan to 5 inches.

fm.Upperside: Gray-brown FW; pink-banded abdomen with dark dorsal stripe
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; powerful migrant; arrives from tropics

hp: Convolvulaceae: Ipomoea (morning glory, sweet potato)

desc: The largest moth in the world by wingspan (up to 12 in). Extremely rare tropical vagrant to SoCal. A few historical records only.

fm.Upperside: White/gray with complex wavy dark lines
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; tropical; extraordinarily rare vagrant
fm.Habitat: Historical vagrant records only; tropical species

hp: Tropical origin.

desc: Salt marsh tiger moth — large white moth with black spots and brilliant orange hindwings and abdomen. The famous 'woolly bear' caterpillar — banded brown-and-black larvae crossing roads in fall. One of LA's most commonly observed caterpillars. 950+ iNat observations in LA County.

fm.Pattern: White FW with black spots; HW orange (♂) or white (♀)
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; to lights; caterpillar is classic "woolly bear"

hp: Polyphagous: many herbaceous plants, corn, soybean, cotton

desc: Aquatic crambid — moth whose larvae live entirely underwater in silk shelters on stream rocks, breathing through skin. One of the most remarkable moth life histories — truly aquatic larvae. Adults small, triangular, attracted to lights near streams. Larvae graze on underwater algae.

fm.Pattern: Brown/tan triangular; dark median band
fm.Behavior: Adults near streams; larvae aquatic — silk tubes on submerged rocks

hp: Freshwater aquatic habitat — streams and ponds. Crambidae.

desc: Small orange-brown micro moth on sage and mint family plants. Both day-active and at lights. Common in coastal sage scrub.

fm.Pattern: Orange with brown margins and spots
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; visits flowers of Lamiaceae

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Coastal habitat. Coastal sage scrub.

desc: Dull brown cutworm moth. Larvae are major agricultural pests — the 'granulate cutworm' that severs seedlings at soil level by night, hiding in soil by day. Attacks virtually all vegetable and field crops. Multiple generations per year in SoCal's warm climate.

fm.Pattern: Dull brown-gray; dark kidney/orbicular spots
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; to lights; larvae are "cutworms" in soil

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Noctuidae. Subterranean.

desc: Orange-and-brown pyralid. Larvae gregarious and conspicuous on Genista (broom), Texas mountain laurel, lupines, and other legumes — feeding communally in silk webs. Orange-brown adult with distinctive pattern. Common in LA gardens with broom plantings.

fm.Pattern: Orange-tan FW; brown HW with pale fringe
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; larvae gregarious, obvious on ornamental Genista

hp: Larvae on Pinus (pines) in montane conifer forest

desc: Voluptuous pyrausta — tiny orange-and-brown micro moth common on native sages. Day-flying and attracted to lights. Larvae on Salvia and Mentha.

fm.Pattern: Orange with brown margins
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; flutters around sage flowers

hp: Mint moth — larvae on Salvia (sage) and Mentha (mint). Small orange-brown crambid on Lamiaceae. Native moth on native plants.

desc: Strikingly colorful micro moth in purple-rose and yellow. One of the most beautiful small moths. On mint family plants.

fm.Pattern: Rose-purple with yellow spots and margins
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; visits mint family flowers

hp: On Rosa (roses) — garden and wild species. Crambidae.

desc: Starred little geometrid — small dark geometrid. Larvae on Asteraceae. Common at lights. Part of the diverse geometrid fauna of SoCal.

fm.Pattern: Dark gray-brown with pale transverse lines
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; common at porch lights

hp: Larvae on asteraceae.

desc: Milkweed crambid — small brown crambid whose larvae feed on milkweed (Asclepias) and ornamental swan plant. Increasing in LA gardens as monarch-friendly milkweed plantings proliferate. An example of a native moth benefiting from conservation plantings intended for another species.

fm.Pattern: Gray-brown; triangular at rest
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; larvae in milkweed flowers/pods

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Narrow-leaf Milkweed.

desc: Marbled plume moth — tiny plume moth on sage. Wings split into feathery plumes held perpendicular to body at rest (T-shape). Distinctive silhouette.

fm.Pattern: Gray-tan; wings split into T-shape plumes
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; distinctive T-shape rest posture

hp: On Salvia (sage) and Lamiaceae. Tiny — easily overlooked. Associated with Black Sage.

desc: Medium noctuid with distinctive silver 'figure-8' mark on forewing. Important crop pest of alfalfa, vegetables, and nursery crops.

fm.Pattern: Mottled brown/gray; silver "U" or figure-8 mark on FW
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; strong flier; to lights and sugar baits

hp: Polyphagous: alfalfa, clover, many crops — looper

desc: Dwarf wave moth — tiny golden-tan geometrid. Larvae feed on coast live oak foliage. One of the smallest geometrids. Common at lights near oaks.

fm.Pattern: Golden-tan with faint darker lines and central dot
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; to lights
fm.Habitat: Oak woodland, urban areas with oaks

hp: Larvae on Quercus agrifolia (coast live oak). Part of oak moth community. Attracted to lights at night.

desc: Small orange-and-brown crambid moth on sage scrub plants. Day-flying. Larvae on sage family. Common in coastal sage scrub.

fm.Color: Orange and brown; day-flying on sage

hp: California mint moth — larvae on native Salvia species in chaparral. Host-specific

desc: Large geometrid with one of the most polyphagous larvae of any moth — feeds on over 50 plant families including avocado, citrus, and ornamentals. Important California pest. Inchworm larvae can strip ornamental plants. Large for a geometrid.

fm.Pattern: Straw-yellow with brown zigzag lines
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; larvae are loopers (inch along) on trees and shrubs
fm.Hosts: Extremely polyphagous — avocado, citrus, roses, oaks

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Sweet Orange. Ant associate.

desc: Small crambid moth whose larvae web and feed on brassica crops (cabbage, broccoli, kale). Can damage seedlings. Pest of cole crops.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Black Mustard.

desc: Metallic silver bilobed mark on forewing (like a figure-8). Related to cabbage looper. Larvae on various crops. Can be agricultural pest.

fm.Pattern: Mottled brown/gray; silver bilobed spot on FW
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; to lights

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Noctuidae. Metallic coloring.

desc: Brown noctuid with pale kidney-shaped mark. One of the most destructive garden pests — larvae cut seedlings at soil level at night.

fm.Pattern: Brown-gray; pale reniform and orbicular spots; variable
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; larvae are climbing cutworms

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Garden associate. In gardens and urban areas. Variegated.

desc: Small brownish tortricid. Larvae roll leaves of many plants including strawberry and ornamentals. Can damage fruit crops. Common pest.

fm.Color: Brown; larvae roll leaves of many plants

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Garden associate. Tortricidae.

desc: Indian meal moth — the most common pantry pest moth worldwide. Diagnostic two-toned forewings: pale inner half, copper-bronze outer half. Larvae infest stored grain, nuts, dried fruit, chocolate, and pet food, spinning silk webbing through provisions. Pheromone traps for monitoring.

fm.Pattern: FW bicolored — inner half cream/gray, outer half copper-red (diagnostic)
fm.Behavior: Found in kitchens/pantries; larvae in stored food
fm.Hosts: Stored grain, cereal, dried fruit, nuts, pet food

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Indian-meal.

desc: Small olive-brown noctuid. Larvae feed on Oxalis (wood sorrel). Very common in gardens year-round. Formerly called 'the cleaver'.

fm.Pattern: Olive-brown with pale transverse lines
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; to lights

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Garden associate. In gardens and urban areas. Wedgling.

desc: Entire-lined crambid — small crambid whose larvae bore in Solanaceae (nightshade family) stems. On tomato, pepper, and native Solanum.

fm.Color: Pale with dark lines; on Solanaceae

hp: Wood/stem borer. Crambidae.

desc: Brown noctuid. Larvae have distinctive yellow dorsal stripe. Significant agricultural pest on many crops. At lights.

fm.Pattern: Brown-gray with paler orbicular and reniform spots
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; larvae gregarious on crops

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Ant associate. Attracted to lights at night. Yellow-striped.

desc: Huge — up to 6 in wingspan. Dark brown/purple. Tropical migrant. Folklore: omen of death in Latin American cultures.

fm.Pattern: Dark brown-purple with eyespots; bat-like in flight
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; powerful migrant; rests on buildings; folklore associations
fm.Habitat: Urban — rests on building walls during migration

hp: Tropical migrant — larvae on Fabaceae (Cassia, Senna, Acacia). One of largest moths in NA (6 in wingspan). Folklore: omen of death.

desc: Large gray sphinx with black dashes on forewings. Larvae on ash, lilac, and privet. Impressive size. Adults at lights.

fm.Pattern: Gray with black dashes on FW; black-and-white banded abdomen
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; powerful hovering flight; to lights

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Arizona Ash. Attracted to lights.

desc: Day-flying sphinx moth with transparent wings resembling a bumblebee. Western counterpart of the eastern Snowberry Clearwing (H. diffinis). Hovers at flowers like a hummingbird, feeding with long proboscis. On honeysuckle and snowberry. Mountain and foothill habitats.

fm.Pattern: Clear wings with dark borders; olive/black fuzzy body
fm.Behavior: Diurnal — hovers at flowers like hummingbird

hp: Caprifoliaceae: Symphoricarpos (snowberry). Visited by/prey for hummingbirds.

desc: Eyed sphinx — large pinkish-gray sphinx moth. When threatened, flashes bright blue eyespots on hindwings — a startling predator deterrent. On willows.

fm.Pattern: Pink-gray FW; HW with blue-black eyespot on pink ground
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; when threatened, flashes HW eyespots

hp: Conservation concern — declining habitat or population. See CONSERVATION status. Associated with Willow.

desc: Coachella giant palm moth — one of the largest erebid moths in North America. Exclusively associated with native California fan palms (Washingtonia filifera). Larvae feed inside palm flower clusters and developing fruit. Named for the Coachella Valley. Desert oases and urban palm plantings.

fm.Pattern: Tan-brown; dark median line; elongated wings
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; found near Washingtonia palms
fm.Habitat: Urban, desert oases — wherever fan palms grow

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Desert habitat. Desert specialist. Large species. Erebidae.

desc: Big poplar sphinx — large sphinx with scalloped hindwing edges. Gray-brown. Larvae feed on poplar and willow. Among the largest moths in LA mountains.

fm.Pattern: Gray-brown with wavy lines; scalloped HW margin
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; adults non-feeding; to lights

hp: Salicaceae: Populus (cottonwood), Salix (willow)

desc: Large sphinx moth with intricate brown, white, and yellow forewing pattern. Tropical species reaching LA in warm years — cannot maintain permanent populations. Larvae on desert willow (Chilopsis) and jasmine. Each warm-year appearance is a colonization event.

fm.Pattern: Complex brown/white/yellow mosaic; yellow HW spots
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; powerful hovering; tropical vagrant/colonizer

hp: Bignoniaceae: desert willow (Chilopsis), jasmine (Jasminum)

desc: Tan noctuid. Larvae are one of the most costly agricultural pests in the Americas, attacking corn, tomatoes, and cotton. Billions in annual damage.

fm.Pattern: Tan to olive-brown; dark spot on FW
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; strong migrant; larvae bore into corn ears, tomatoes

hp: Polyphagous: corn (silk/ear), tomato, cotton, lettuce

desc: Gray bark-patterned forewings conceal bright orange and black hindwings. Flash display startles predators when moth takes flight from tree trunk.

fm.Pattern: Gray bark-like FW; orange-red HW with black bands
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; rests on oak bark — nearly invisible; flashes bright HW in flight

hp: Aerial predator of flying insects. Associated with Ash.

desc: Tiny cosmopolitan pest of brassicas (cabbage, broccoli, kale). When wings folded, three pale diamonds along back. Evolves insecticide resistance rapidly.

fm.Pattern: Gray-brown; pale diamond-shapes visible along back when folded
fm.Behavior: Can be extremely abundant; migrant; pesticide-resistant

hp: Brassicaceae: cabbage, kale, broccoli, canola — global pest

desc: Tiny moth whose larvae mine serpentine trails inside citrus leaves, creating visible silvery winding tracks. Major pest since its arrival in SoCal around 2000. Damaged leaves curl and distort. Controlled by the parasitoid Cirrospilus near universalis.

fm.Pattern: White/silver with dark streaks; fringed wings
fm.Behavior: Larvae mine distinctive serpentine trails visible in citrus leaves

hp: Pest of Citrus. Larvae mine or bore in citrus foliage/fruit. On Citrus (citrus). Parasitoid.

desc: Slender moth with wings deeply split into feathery plumes. T-shaped at rest. Common on morning glory and bindweed. Cosmopolitan.

fm.Pattern: Pale tan; wings split into plumes; T-shape at rest
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; rests with wings in T

hp: Convolvulaceae: bindweed (Convolvulus), morning glory

desc: Larvae build enormous communal silk webs engulfing entire tree branches. White adult moth. Native despite being called a pest.

fm.Pattern: White; sometimes with dark spots
fm.Behavior: Larvae build conspicuous communal web tents on trees
fm.Hosts: Polyphagous — willows, mulberry, walnut, many trees

hp: Polyphagous: 100+ tree species inc. Mulberry, walnut, maple

desc: Western tent caterpillar — larvae build communal silken tents in tree forks each spring — a familiar sight on coast live oaks, willows, and ornamentals. Adults are stocky tan-brown moths. Periodic outbreaks defoliate trees. Caterpillars bask communally in tent for thermoregulation.

fm.Pattern: Tan-brown; two pale diagonal FW lines
fm.Behavior: Larvae in communal silk tents in tree forks; spring defoliator

hp: Quercus (oaks), Salix (willows), Populus, Rosa. Communal silk tent on host tree.

desc: Striking: scarlet FW with gray-blue stripes. Larvae feed on lichens growing on tree bark. Frequently at lights.

fm.Pattern: Scarlet-red FW with gray-blue stripes
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; to lights; larvae on lichens

hp: Lichens and algae on tree bark — not plant-feeding

desc: Day-flying dark moth with diagnostic yellow-orange collar band. Metallic blue-black wings. Wasp mimic — narrow-waisted body and deliberate flight enhance the resemblance. Visits flowers. Larvae on grasses. One of the more commonly seen day-flying moths.

fm.Pattern: Metallic blue-black; yellow-orange collar/neck
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; visits flowers; wasp mimic

hp: Open grassland. Display flights in courtship. On Poaceae (grasses). Metallic coloring.

desc: Day-flying buck moth related to H. electra. Desert populations. Stinging caterpillar. Male yellow, female gray-pink.

fm.Pattern: ♂: yellow with black eye spot. ♀: gray-pink.
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; stinging caterpillar

hp: Giant silk moths. Larvae on trees. Adults non-feeding. Stinging caterpillars in some species. Preys on caterpillar.

desc: Gray moth named for the dagger-shaped dark mark on forewing. Caterpillar is spectacular — bright yellow covered in dense tufts of black bristle-like hairs (tussocks). On many deciduous trees. The caterpillar is far more commonly noticed than the adult.

fm.Pattern: Gray; dark dagger-shaped FW mark
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; showy caterpillar often more noticed than adult

hp: Larvae spectacular — bright yellow with dense white hair tufts. On hardwood trees (Acer, Quercus, Betula). Gray dagger-marked adults at lights.

desc: Large gray sphinx moth with elegant wavy dark forewing lines. Visits flowers at dusk with hovering flight. Larvae (hornworms) on ash and privet — similar to tomato hornworm but on different hosts. Common at porch lights in summer. Impressive size.

fm.Pattern: Gray with wavy dark and pale FW lines
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; hovers at flowers

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Arizona Ash. Ant associate.

desc: Sleek, streamlined tropical sphinx moth with horn-like head projections. Green-gray with elegant diagonal lines. Reaches SoCal as a summer immigrant from tropical Mexico. Larvae on Pentas and Rubiaceae. Any LA sighting is a noteworthy range extension.

fm.Pattern: Olive-brown; streamlined; distinctive triangular head
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; powerful migrant from tropics

hp: Larvae on pentas and rubiaceae. Migratory. Tropical origin.

desc: Huge yellow moth with purple-brown patches. One of largest NA moths. Very rare vagrant to SoCal at extreme western range edge.

fm.Pattern: Yellow with purple-brown patches and spots
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; adults non-feeding; to lights

hp: Giant silk moths. Larvae on trees. Adults non-feeding. Stinging caterpillars in some species. Ant associate.

desc: Silver comma-mark on forewing like related Autographa species. Larvae (semi-loopers) on lettuce, celery, and many crops. Common at lights. The metallic silver mark is diagnostic of the plusiine group — metallic spots are structurally colored, not pigmented.

fm.Pattern: Gray-brown; silver comma/falcate mark on FW
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; to lights; larvae are loopers

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Attracted to lights. Attracted to lights at night. Metallic coloring.

desc: Geometrid with bark-mimicking cryptic pattern in mottled gray-brown. Virtually invisible on oak bark. Wingspan variable. Larvae feed on oaks. The precise bark-matching camouflage is remarkable — wings replicate bark texture, lichens, and crevice patterns.

fm.Pattern: Mottled gray-brown; bark-mimicking; variable
fm.Behavior: Rests on oak bark — nearly invisible

hp: Larvae on Quercus agrifolia (coast live oak). Part of oak moth community. Mimicry specialist.

desc: Day-flying black-and-white crambid visiting flowers in mountain meadows. Bold white spots on black wings. Larvae on goldenrod. The day-flying habit and bold pattern distinguish it from the mostly nocturnal and drab pyraloid moths.

fm.Pattern: Black with white spots; day-flying
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; visits flowers; distinctive pattern

hp: Nocturnal predator. White-spotted.

desc: Small straw-colored crambid. Larvae create a distinctive cross-striped pattern of feeding damage on Brassicaceae crops — transverse bands of skeletonized tissue. Can cause significant damage to cabbage, broccoli, and kale in LA gardens.

fm.Pattern: Straw-yellow with dark cross-lines
fm.Behavior: Nocturnal; larvae are cross-striped on crucifers

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Black Mustard. Garden associate.

desc: Small black-and-white crambid. Larvae web and feed on beet, spinach, amaranth, and related Chenopodiaceae leaves. Pantropical distribution — widespread in warm regions. The recurved wing posture at rest gives the species its name. Common garden pest.

fm.Pattern: Dark brown with white transverse bands
fm.Behavior: Larvae web leaves; common on Amaranthaceae

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Garden associate. Family Crambidae. Common species. In gardens and urban areas.

desc: Grape leaffolder — striking black moth with bold white forewing spots. Larvae fold and web grape leaves, feeding inside the shelter. Day-active occasionally — unusual for a pyraloid. The black-and-white pattern is bold and distinctive for such a small moth.

fm.Pattern: Black with 2 white spots per wing
fm.Behavior: Larvae roll grape/Virginia creeper leaves into tubes

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Crambidae.

desc: Tiny, pale moth abundant in grass. Often flushed when walking through lawn. One of the most common micro-moths.

fm.Pattern: Pale tan; tiny dark FW spots
fm.Behavior: Flushed from grass when walking; to lights

hp: Moth — larvae on host plant foliage or stems. Adults nocturnal, at lights.

desc: Medium crambid with distinctive elongated resting posture. Extremely common at lights. Larvae on legumes/grasses.

fm.Pattern: Gray-brown; elongated wings held tight at rest
fm.Behavior: Abundant at lights; rests with wings wrapped tight

hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Associated with Legumes.

desc: Mason's flower moth — colorful noctuid that rests inside flower heads during the day, its green, pink, and cream coloring precisely matching the flower. One of the most remarkably camouflaged moths — invisible inside a bloom. Each Schinia species matches its specific host flower.

fm.Pattern: Green, pink, and cream — matches Asteraceae flower heads
fm.Behavior: Day-resting on flower heads; perfectly camouflaged

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Noctuidae. Brightly colored.

desc: Tiny white pug — small white geometrid. Unusual among moths — larvae eat flowers rather than leaves. Very common but rarely noticed due to small size and plain appearance. Part of the enormous Eupithecia genus with over 1,400 species — the largest genus in Geometridae.

fm.Pattern: White with faint gray markings; tiny
fm.Behavior: To lights; larvae eat flowers/buds (florivorous)

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Geometridae. Very small species.

desc: Small, unusual moth whose caterpillar constructs a portable leaf case. Uncommon family — only a few NA species.

fm.Pattern: Tan-brown with scalloped wing edges
fm.Behavior: Caterpillar builds portable leaf case — carries it while feeding

hp: Case moth — caterpillar constructs portable leaf case. Uncommon LA County species on native trees. Family Mimallonidae — small and rarely seen.

desc: Tiny orange-and-brown crambid similar to P. inornatalis. On native sages (Salvia) and mints (Lamiaceae). Day-flying — unusual for a pyraloid. The orange coloring and diurnal habit make this one of the more conspicuous small moths in chaparral.

fm.Pattern: Orange with brown margins
fm.Behavior: Diurnal; flutters near sage flowers

hp: Coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Associated with Black Sage. Associated with Mints.

desc: Large silkmoth of mountain pine forests. Periodic outbreaks defoliate pines. Larvae edible — Native American food source.
hp: Pinaceae: Pinus ponderosa, P. Jeffreyi, P. Contorta

desc: Brown sphinx moth with stunning blue-and-pink hindwing eyespots revealed when forewings part (startle display). Larvae on cherry, plum, and other Rosaceae. Nocturnal. Named for Myops ('small eye'), referring to the hindwing spots. Foothill orchards and native Prunus.
hp: Nocturnal predator. Associated with Hollyleaf Cherry.

fm.Wings: Brown sphinx moth with blue and pink hindwing eyespots

desc: Olive and pink sphinx moth. Larvae boldly marked with yellow spots on dark body, feeding on Galium (bedstraw) and Epilobium (fireweed). Day- and dusk-flying. Holarctic distribution — one of few moths found across NA, Europe, and Asia. Uncommon in LA.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Sphingidae.

desc: California oakworm — small moth with translucent, semi-transparent wings. Larvae periodically defoliate coast live oaks — LA's most important tree pest. Endemic to California — found nowhere else on Earth. Outbreaks strip oak canopy, raining frass. Two broods per year.
hp: Quercus agrifolia (coast live oak) — endemic CA specialist

desc: Small tortricid moth and significant pest of California citrus, grapes, and ornamentals. Larvae roll and web leaves, feeding inside the shelter. Year-round in SoCal with overlapping generations. Named for San Francisco. Biological control using Trichogramma wasps effective in IPM programs.
hp: Polyphagous: citrus, apple, rose, many ornamentals

desc: Western spring cankerworm — females completely wingless (unusual among moths). Larvae are the classic 'inchworms' defoliating oaks in spring. Males fly to lights. Western counterpart of the eastern P. vernata. Periodic outbreaks can strip oak canopy in foothill canyons.
hp: Larvae defoliate oaks. Can cause significant canopy damage. On Quercus (oak). Canyon habitat.

desc: The most economically important pest of California tree nuts, causing over $100M annual losses. Larvae bore into almonds, walnuts, and pistachios, and can vector aflatoxin-producing Aspergillus fungi. Gray-brown moth with crescent forewing mark. Year-round in LA's warm climate.
hp: Larvae feed inside nut shells.

fm.Wings: Major pest of almonds, walnuts, and citrus

desc: Noctuid moth expanding range westward into CA. Larvae damage corn and bean crops by cutting seedlings at soil level. Agricultural concern.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Noctuidae. Western.

desc: Virgin tiger moth — striking arctiiid with dark forewings featuring white veins and vivid pink-red hindwings with dark spots. Mountain meadows, chaparral, and open woodland. Larvae densely woolly (woolly bear type). The pink hindwings flash as a startle display when disturbed. Western NA endemic.
hp: Polyphagous: various low-growing herbs

fm.Color: Dark forewings with white veins; pink hindwings

desc: Pest of beehives whose larvae consume beeswax combs. Also extensively used as a model organism in immunology and pharmacology research — wax moth larvae are widely used to test antimicrobial compounds, replacing some mammalian testing.
hp: Apis mellifera: beeswax, pollen, honey — beehive pest

desc: Caterpillar builds a portable case (bag) from silk, plant debris, and twigs, carrying it while feeding. Bags hang from trees like small ornaments. Female wingless and larviform — never leaves the bag. Males small and dark, fly to bags for mating.

fm.Wings: Caterpillar builds portable case from silk and plant debris

hp: Larvae build bag from silk and plant debris, carrying it while feeding on tree foliage.

desc: Snowy eupseudosoma — white tussock moth whose larvae are remarkable velvet ant mimics — orange and black with dense spiny hairs and a raised posterior tuft resembling a stinger. The mimicry deters predators who mistake the caterpillar for a painfully stinging mutillid wasp.

fm.Wings: White moth whose larvae mimic velvet ants with orange and black coloring and faux stinger

hp: Caterpillar predator — hunts moth and butterfly larvae. Preys on caterpillar. Spiny/armed.

desc: Devastating defoliator. Males brown and fly; females white and flightless. Subject of active eradication programs.
hp: Spongy moth — devastating defoliator of 500+ tree species. Subject of massive eradication campaigns. Not established in SoCal but under surveillance.

desc: European sphinx deliberately introduced to NA for biological control of leafy spurge (Euphorbia esula). Colorful caterpillars with red, yellow, and black markings on spurge. The deliberate introduction for weed control is a classic biocontrol story.
hp: Biological control agent — predator of caterpillars. Preys on caterpillar.

desc: Five-spotted hawk moth — large gray sphinx moth. Caterpillar is the notorious tomato hornworm — green with white diagonal stripes and prominent posterior horn. Major pest of tomato, pepper, and eggplant. Adult has five orange spots along each side of abdomen (diagnostic).
hp: Leaf miner on host plant. Caterpillar predator.

desc: Peachtree borer — clearwing moth with transparent wings mimicking a wasp. Larva bores into peach and plum trunks at ground level, causing gummosis (resin exudation). Important stone fruit pest. The wasp mimicry is convincing — complete with narrow waist and dark wings.
hp: Peachtree borer — clearwing wasp-mimic. Larvae bore into Prunus (peach, plum, cherry) trunk base. Major stone fruit pest.

fm.Color: Clear wings; wasp mimic with blue-black body

desc: Forked tortrix — small tortricid whose larvae roll grape and other leaves. Minor vineyard pest. Part of the diverse leafroller moth community.

fm.Wings: Small tortricid moth whose larvae roll grape leaves

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Tortricidae.

desc: Small gray tortricid and major pest of stone fruit (peach, nectarine, almond). Larvae bore into twigs (causing flagging) and ripening fruit. One of the most important pests in California almond and peach orchards. Controlled by mating disruption and Bt.
hp: Peach twig borer on Prunus (peach, nectarine, almond). Larvae bore in new shoots, later enter fruit. IPM target in CA orchards.

desc: Historically the most devastating cotton pest. Pink larvae bore into cotton bolls. The first genetically modified crop (Bt cotton) was developed specifically to combat this species. Now largely eradicated from US through Bt cotton and sterile insect programs.
hp: Malvaceae: cotton (Gossypium) — Bt cotton developed for control

desc: Australian tortricid detected in California in 2007. CDFA quarantine pest of many fruit and vegetable crops. Subject of controversial eradication efforts. Can feed on over 500 plant species. Any detection triggers regulatory response.
hp: Polyphagous: apple, grape, citrus, many crops and ornamentals

desc: Juniper looper — brown geometrid whose inchworm larvae can defoliate juniper and other conifers. Sometimes a significant pest. Desert margins.

fm.Wings: Brown geometrid moth whose inchworm larvae feed on juniper foliage

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with California Juniper.

desc: Small orange moth with white-spotted forewings. Larvae web and feed on tree-of-heaven (Ailanthus altissima) leaves. Originally native to tropical Sapindaceae, the moth has expanded its range dramatically following the spread of invasive Ailanthus across NA.
hp: Introduced species. Invasive.

fm.Wings: Small orange moth with white-spotted wings

desc: Distinctive T-shaped moth with deeply divided, feather-like wings (diagnostic of Pterophoridae). Larvae bore into artichoke heads and thistle buds — specialized pest. The T-shape at rest is unmistakable. Named for its thistle (Carduus) association.
hp: Artichoke plume moth — T-shaped feathery wings. Larvae bore in Cynara (artichoke) and Cirsium (thistle) heads. Asteraceae specialist.

desc: Pale green woodling — green-gray noctuid. Larvae are cutworms cutting plant stems at soil level. Adults remarkably long-lived — overwinter as adults.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Ant associate. Noctuidae.

desc: White furcula — white prominent moth. Caterpillars are bright green with a bizarre forked tail held upright — when threatened, they extrude long red filaments from the fork tips as a startle display. On cherry, willow, and other deciduous trees. The tail display is remarkable.
hp: Salicaceae: willows (Salix), poplars (Populus).

desc: Black cutworm — dark noctuid whose larvae are among the most destructive cutworm pests worldwide. Larvae sever seedlings at soil level — can destroy entire crop stands overnight. Highly migratory — reinvades northern regions annually. Year-round in SoCal.
hp: Polyphagous: seedlings of many crops and garden plants

desc: Yellow moth with dark spots arranged in rows. Caterpillars covered in spectacular tufts of black and yellow hair on willows and other trees. The hairy caterpillars are among the most commonly photographed LA larvae. Common at summer lights.
hp: Salicaceae: willows (Salix), poplars (Populus).

desc: Gray prominent moth. Larvae gregarious on oaks — can completely defoliate trees during periodic outbreaks, giving affected groves a scorched appearance. Named for the Manteo people. Larvae have a distinctive horn on the eighth abdominal segment.
hp: Fagaceae: oaks (Quercus) — can defoliate during outbreaks

desc: Hemlock looper — brown geometrid whose inchworm larvae periodically defoliate conifers in major outbreaks. In western forests, attacks firs and hemlocks. Periodic outbreak cycles can strip mountain conifer canopy over thousands of acres. Important forest pest with boom-bust dynamics.
hp: Conifers: hemlock, fir, spruce

desc: Brown noctuid migrating to mountains in summer. Called miller moth when abundant. Important food for grizzly bears.
hp: Polyphagous: alfalfa, wheat, many crops and grasses

fm.Habitat: Agricultural areas; mountain summits in summer

desc: Metallic blue day-flying wasp-mimic moth with vivid orange head. Larvae on grasses. The striking combination of metallic blue wings, orange head, and narrow waist creates a convincing wasp disguise. One of the most beautiful day-flying moths in LA County.
hp: Mimicry — Batesian or aggressive. Mimicry specialist. Metallic coloring.

fm.Color: Metallic blue body; dark wings; orange head

desc: Small tan geometrid whose inchworm larvae have bizarre filament-like projections. On many trees. Common at lights. Unusual larval form.
hp: Geometrid moth — inchworm larvae on trees/shrubs. Bird food. Small species. Attracted to lights. Attracted to lights at night.

desc: Fall armyworm — gray-brown noctuid whose larvae devastate corn, grasses, and many crops in mass migratory outbreaks. Cannot overwinter in cold areas — reinvades northward each year. A global emergency pest since 2016 when it invaded Africa and Asia from the Americas.
hp: Polyphagous: corn, grasses, sorghum, rice, many crops

desc: Gray noctuid. Larvae with bold yellow-and-black stripes — serious pest of vegetables, alfalfa, and field crops in western NA. Can march in army-like mass movements when food is depleted. Important agricultural pest in California.
hp: Polyphagous: alfalfa, sugar beets, tomato, many crops

desc: Yellow scallop moth — brown noctuid with scalloped hindwing margin. Larvae on Malvaceae (cotton family) including native mallows and ornamental hibiscus. Increasingly common in LA with climate warming — historically more tropical. Adults with distinctive hooked forewing tips.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Cheeseweed.

desc: Large green sphinx moth with pink-brown bands. Fast powerful flier visiting flowers at dusk. Larvae on evening primrose. Occasional visitor to SoCal.
hp: Onagraceae: Oenothera (evening primrose), Gaura

desc: Great Basin tent caterpillar — close relative of M. californicum. Communal tent-building caterpillars on desert willows, cottonwoods, and other shrubs. Desert and desert-edge habitats. Tents smaller than californicum. Important in desert riparian food webs.
hp: Rosaceae, Salicaceae: willows, wild rose, mountain mahogany

desc: Brown noctuid and major soybean pest. Migratory — reaches SoCal in warm years from subtropical breeding areas. Larvae can devastate soybean fields in major outbreaks. The first Bt transgenic crop (soybean) was developed partly to combat this species.
hp: Larvae devastate soybean (Glycine max) and other Fabaceae. Migratory — cannot overwinter in SoCal. Important agricultural pest when populations arrive.

desc: Joined-line crambid — small gray pyralid whose larvae bore into mesquite (Prosopis) seed pods. Desert species reaching LA County margins from the Mojave. Part of the mesquite-associated insect community — an important guild in desert food webs.
hp: Wood/stem borer. Associated with Honey Mesquite.

desc: Caterpillars march in processions. Urticating hairs cause severe dermatitis. European — under surveillance in CA.
hp: Larvae processionary on oaks. Urticating hairs — severe dermatitis. European quarantine concern.

desc: Very common small tan geometrid at every porch light. Inchworm larvae on a wide range of plants. Often the most numerous moth at lights.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Attracted to lights. Family Geometridae. Common species. Attracted to lights at night.

desc: Glover's silk moth — large reddish-brown saturniid. Western relative of the famous cecropia moth. Impressive 4-5 inch wingspan. Larvae on willow, cherry, and mountain mahogany. Crescent wing marks. Adults do not feed — live only a few days. Mountain habitats.
hp: Rosaceae: cherry, plum; Salicaceae: willow; Cercocarpus

desc: Parental richia — small brown noctuid of desert edges. Larvae on various low plants. At lights in arid habitats. Part of western desert moth fauna.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Desert habitat. Desert specialist. Arizona.

desc: Mottled gray moth with intricate markings resembling handwritten script or calligraphy. Larvae on Rubus (blackberry). Related to hook-tip moths (Drepanidae). The script-like markings are diagnostic and give the species its name ('scripta' = written).
hp: Rosaceae: Rubus (blackberry, raspberry)

desc: Brown arches — rich brown noctuid with curved dark lines and a warm pink tinge to the forewings. Larvae polyphagous on herbaceous plants. At lights and sugar baits. The warm coloring and elegant curved lines make this one of the more attractive noctuids in LA.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Ant associate. Attracted to lights at night. Curved-lined.

desc: Fuzzy gray-brown moth resembling a small piece of bark or a dead leaf. Larvae on cherry, ash, and other deciduous trees. Related to silk moths (Bombycoidea) but much smaller. The furry, bark-like appearance provides excellent camouflage on tree trunks.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with California Wild Rose.

desc: Tiny T-shaped plume moth. Larvae on snapdragon and toadflax. Distinctive rolled wings at rest give all plume moths their feathery appearance.
hp: Snapdragon plume moth — T-shaped plume moth whose larvae feed on Antirrhinum (snapdragon) and Linaria (toadflax). Plantaginaceae specialist.

desc: Pale plume moth on yarrow (Achillea). Found mainland and Catalina Island. Larvae bore into yarrow stems and flower heads.
hp: Plume moth on native plants. Feathery divided wings. Wood/stem borer.

desc: White plume moth associated with Baccharis (coyote brush). Malibu specimen records. Larvae feed on coyote brush flower heads.
hp: Plume moth on native plants. Feathery divided wings. On Baccharis (coyote brush).

desc: Tiny introduced moth with distinctive yellow V mark on dark wings. Larvae damage wine corks and dried foods. Originally from Mediterranean.

fm.Color: Dark brown-black with yellow V mark

hp: Introduced species. Tineidae.

desc: Small bright green sphinx moth of coastal sage scrub. Day-flying — hovers at flowers. Green fades to yellow in preserved specimens. California near-endemic.

fm.Color: Bright green FW (fades in collection); dark HW

hp: In coastal sage scrub habitat. Associated with Black Sage. Diurnal/day-active.

desc: Males gray with faint crossbands; females wingless. Larvae are common 'inchworms' on deciduous trees. Emerge after first fall rains.

fm.Color: ♂ gray with faint bands; ♀ wingless, gray-brown

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Geometridae. Fall.

desc: Small tan geometrid. Like fall cankerworm, ♀ is wingless. Larvae feed on oak and other hardwoods. Can outbreak and defoliate.
hp: Larvae defoliate oaks. Can cause significant canopy damage. On Quercus (oak). On oaks (Quercus).

desc: Small pale geometrid with fine dark speckling. Larvae on currants and gooseberries. Common at lights throughout LA suburbs.

fm.Color: Pale cream-yellow with fine brown speckling

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Golden Currant.

desc: Small narrow moth that rests with wings wrapped around body like a tube. Larvae damage turf grass. The moth you flush from lawns when walking at dusk.

fm.Habitat: Turf grass, lawns — larvae damage roots

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Crambidae.

desc: Small olive-brown crambid with wavy pale crosslines. Larvae web together alfalfa and beet leaves. Very common at lights in agricultural areas.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Attracted to lights. Attracted to lights at night. Alfalfa.

desc: Dull brown concealer moth found indoors. Larvae eat wool, fur, feathers, dried food, and even book bindings. Originally from Asia.
hp: Concealer moths — larvae in leaf litter and dead plant material. Oecophoridae.

desc: Variable brown noctuid with dark C-mark on forewing. Larvae are common garden cutworms. One of the most frequently photographed moths at LA blacklights.

fm.Color: Brown with dark C/dagger mark on FW

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Garden associate. Family Noctuidae. Common species. In gardens and urban areas.

desc: Pale gray geometrid with wavy crosslines. Very common at lights. Larvae on many hardwoods. One of the 'LBMs' (little brown moths) that dominate LA blacklight sheets.

fm.Color: Pale gray with wavy dark crosslines

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Attracted to lights. Family Geometridae. Common species. Pale-winged.

desc: Small bright yellow geometrid with brown FW border. Day-flying — frequents flowers. One of the few geometrids active by day. Larvae on honey locust.

fm.Color: Bright yellow with brown FW border

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Geometridae. Common.

desc: Pink-and-yellow geometrid — strikingly colorful for a moth. Larvae feed on chickweed and clover. Common at lights. The 'cherry blossom moth.'

fm.Color: Pink bands on yellow ground — very colorful

hp: On clover (Trifolium) and related legumes (Fabaceae). Associated with Cherry.

desc: Large yellow-tan geometrid with pointed wing tips. Single dark line across both wings. Very common at lights. Larvae on many trees.

fm.Color: Yellow-tan; single dark diagonal crossline; pointed FW tips

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Attracted to lights. Family Geometridae. Common species. Attracted to lights at night.

desc: Black forewings with cream lines; orange-pink hindwings with black spots. Classic tiger moth pattern. Larvae ('woolly bears') feed on many low plants.

fm.Color: Black FW with cream lines; orange-pink HW with black spots
fm.Habitat: Grassland, chaparral — larvae on low plants

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Erebidae.

desc: Small tan crambid with dark markings. Larvae web together leaves of beans, beets, and many garden plants. Very common at lights.

fm.Color: Tan with dark brown spots and lines
fm.Habitat: Vegetable gardens — larvae web leaves together

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Garden associate. Family Crambidae. Common species. In gardens and urban areas.

desc: Tiny gold-tan moth. The classic fabric-eating clothes moth — larvae spin silken tubes in wool, fur, feathers. Weak flier that avoids light (unlike pantry moths).

fm.Habitat: Closets — larvae eat wool, silk, fur, feathers
fm.Behavior: Avoids light (unlike pantry moths); runs rather than flies

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Tineidae. Very small species.

desc: Similar to webbing clothes moth but with three dark spots on each FW. Larvae build portable silk cases. Damages woolens, furs, and taxidermy.

fm.Color: Brown-tan with 3 dark FW spots (vs. plain in webbing moth)
fm.Habitat: Closets, museums — larvae in portable silk cases

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Tineidae. Casemaking.

desc: Pale green-white with brown patch and white dot. Larvae spectacular — green with yellow side stripe on oak. Classic prominent moth shape at rest (tented wings).

fm.Color: Pale green-white; brown FW patch with white dot

hp: Leaf miner on host plant. Associated with Coast Live Oak.

desc: Dark noctuid with pale crossband. Larvae are striking — velvety black with yellow lateral stripes. Pest of ornamentals and vegetables.

fm.Color: Dark brown with pale median band
fm.Habitat: Gardens — larvae velvety black with yellow stripes

hp: Pest of ornamentals and.

desc: Cryptic bark-pattern forewings conceal brilliant orange-and-black hindwings — revealed in flash-and-vanish startle display. Larvae on oak. One of several CA Catocala species.

fm.Color: Bark-pattern FW; brilliant orange + black HW
fm.Habitat: Oak woodland — cryptic at rest on bark
fm.Behavior: Flash-and-vanish: reveals bright HW when startled

hp: On oak bark. Part of bark-dwelling arthropod community. Under bark. Cryptically colored.

desc: Tiny straw-colored moth that rests with wings wrapped tightly around body. Extremely common in lawns — the moth flushed by every footstep at dusk.

fm.Habitat: Turf grass — extremely common in lawns
fm.Behavior: Flushed en masse when walking through grass at dusk

hp: Larvae in grass stems, rolled leaves, or aquatic. Diverse ecological roles.

desc: Spectacular wasp mimic with clear wings — one of the most convincing mimicry systems in insects. Larvae bore in sycamore. Day-flying. Often mistaken for a yellowjacket.

fm.Behavior: Day-flying wasp mimic; remarkably convincing

hp: On western sycamore (platanus racemosa). On Platanus (sycamore). Mimicry specialist.

desc: Large heavy-bodied moth with mottled bark-pattern wings. Larvae bore 1-inch tunnels through living hardwood trunks. 3-year larval cycle. Can damage structural trees.

fm.Habitat: Hardwood trees — larvae bore large tunnels in trunk

hp: Under bark — wood-boring community. Wood/stem borer.

desc: Dark mottled cutworm moth. Larvae feed on roots and stems of many garden plants. Very common at blacklights. One of the most frequently encountered noctuids in urban LA.

fm.Color: Dark brown mottled; pale reniform spot

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Garden associate. Family Noctuidae. Common species. In gardens and urban areas.

desc: Pale tan-brown with single white dot on each FW. Larvae march in armies across fields (hence name). Major pest of turf and grain crops. Migratory from Mexico.

fm.Color: Pale tan-brown; single white dot on each FW
fm.Habitat: Turf, grain — larvae march in groups

hp: Stored product pest of grain and dried foods. Noctuidae.

desc: Small gray-brown noctuid with kidney-shaped (reniform) spot. Very common at lights. Larvae feed on clover, alfalfa, and many garden plants.
hp: Alfalfa associate — larvae or adults feed on alfalfa (Medicago sativa). In gardens and urban areas.

desc: Small triangular moth with pointed snout (labial palps). Larvae are green 'clover worms' on legumes. Very common at lights — one of the first moths seen at any LA blacklight.

fm.Color: Brown; pointed snout; triangular at rest

hp: On clover (Trifolium) and related legumes (Fabaceae). Attracted to lights at night.

desc: Small gray-brown litter moth with wavy crosslines. Larvae feed on dead leaves and decaying wood. Among the most numerically abundant moths at blacklights.

fm.Habitat: Woodland — larvae in leaf litter and dead wood

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Ant associate. Erebidae.

desc: Small dark brown owlet with slanted crosslines. Feeds on dead leaves and fungi on the forest floor. Common at lights in foothill oak woodland.
hp: Larvae on Quercus agrifolia (coast live oak). Part of oak moth community. Oak woodland habitat.

desc: Small pale geometrid with sharp-angled crosslines. Very common at lights spring through fall. Larvae on many hardwoods. Subtle but frequent.

fm.Color: Pale tan with sharp dark crosslines at angle

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Attracted to lights. Family Geometridae. Common species. Attracted to lights at night.

desc: Delicate pale cream geometrid with scalloped brown wing borders. One of the most elegant small moths. Larvae on dandelion, chickweed. Common in gardens.

fm.Color: Pale cream with scalloped brown borders — lacy appearance
fm.Habitat: Gardens, weedy areas — common at lights

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Garden associate. In gardens and urban areas. Large.

desc: Small brown crambid with translucent wing patches. Larvae web leaves of beets, chard, spinach. Pantropical species — common in SoCal gardens.

fm.Color: Brown with translucent patches and dark spots

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Garden associate. Family Crambidae. Common species. In gardens and urban areas.

desc: Tiny gray-brown moth. Larvae mine potato tubers and tomato/eggplant leaves. Major stored-potato pest worldwide. Common in LA home gardens and warehouses.

fm.Color: Narrow gray-brown wings with dark speckling
fm.Habitat: Potato, tomato, eggplant — mines tubers and leaves

hp: Leaf miner on host plant. Garden associate.

desc: Pale tan dart moth. Larvae are one of the most destructive cutworms in western agriculture — cut grain seedlings underground, destroying entire fields. Serious pest of wheat and barley. Named 'orthogonia' for the right-angled wing markings.
hp: Stored product pest of grain and dried foods. On wheat.

desc: Olive-shaded bird-dropping moth — tiny noctuid with extraordinary bird-dropping camouflage. White-and-brown splotchy pattern so convincingly mimics avian excrement that it is virtually invisible on leaf surfaces. On composites. One of nature's most remarkable camouflage strategies.
hp: Extraordinary bird-dropping mimic — white-and-brown splotch pattern. Tiny noctuid on Ambrosia (ragweed) and Helianthus (sunflower). Asteraceae specialist.

desc: Master dart — gray-brown dart moth with pinkish reniform (kidney-shaped) stigma spot. Common at blacklights. Larvae are cutworms that damage gardens and crops by severing seedlings at soil level. Multiple generations per year in LA's warm climate. Part of the large cutworm complex.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Garden associate. Family Noctuidae. Common species. In gardens and urban areas.

desc: Pale straw-colored noctuid with distinctive dark dots along wing veins. Larvae on grasses. Common at lights year-round.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. On Poaceae (grasses). Attracted to lights at night.

desc: Striking white forewings edged olive-brown and lilac. At rest, resembles a bird dropping or patch of lichen on bark. Larvae on grape and Virginia creeper. The unusual wing shape and coloring produce remarkably effective cryptic camouflage.
hp: Larvae feed on lichens growing on rocks and tree bark. Lichen moth. On grape.

desc: Tan-brown prominent moth with dark streaks. Larvae gregarious — colonies of black-and-yellow-striped caterpillars that arch their heads and tails upward simultaneously when disturbed, creating a striking group defense display.
hp: Gregarious larvae on walnut, birch, oak. Communal defensive head-arching display.

desc: Camouflaged looper — small emerald-green geometrid. Larvae perform remarkable living camouflage — attach flower petals, leaf fragments, and plant material to body with silk, becoming virtually invisible on their food plants. One of the most remarkable larval camouflage strategies in any insect.
hp: Inchworm decorates body with live flower petals for camouflage — one of few insects using external living material (Heinrich 1979).

desc: Bent-lined gray — pale gray geometrid with diagnostic sharp, angulated dark crossline. Very common at lights spring through fall — one of the most frequently encountered small moths in LA. Despite abundance, subtle pattern is overlooked. Larvae on various low plants.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Attracted to lights. Family Geometridae. Common species. Bent-line.

desc: Small olive-brown crambid. Larvae web and tie celery, beet, and greenhouse plant leaves. Very common at lights year-round in LA's mild climate — one of the most frequently encountered small moths. Multiple overlapping generations.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Ant associate. Attracted to lights at night. Celery.

desc: Small gray pyralid and major flour mill pest worldwide. Larvae spin silk through flour, creating dense webbing that clogs machinery. Can infest entire mills. Named for its association with milling. Controlled through fumigation and pheromone traps.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Mediterranean.

desc: Small gray pyralid. Major California dried fruit pest — larvae infest raisins, dates, and figs. Can destroy stored dried fruit in warehouses.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Pyralidae.

desc: Small brown bell-shaped tortricid. Larvae roll leaves of grape, citrus, and 100+ other plant species — one of the most polyphagous tortricids in California. Important vineyard and citrus pest. The rolled leaf shelter is diagnostic of the larval feeding habit.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Sweet Orange. Ant associate.

desc: Fruittree leafroller — variable tan tortricid. Larvae damage apple, cherry, and citrus by rolling leaves and feeding on fruit surface. Important orchard pest.
hp: Pest of Citrus. Larvae mine or bore in citrus foliage/fruit. Associated with Cherry.

desc: Snowberry sphinx — pale gray sphinx moth of clean, elegant appearance. Larvae on snowberry (Symphoricarpos) in mountain chaparral. Distinguished from other gray sphingids by the unusually uniform, unstreaked pale gray forewings. Uncommon — habitat-specific to montane shrubland.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Common Pear. Chaparral habitat.

desc: Spectacular wasp-mimic moth with metallic green forewings, bright orange abdomen and legs, and clear hindwings. Day-flying. Larvae bore inside squash vine stems, causing sudden wilting and death. The convincing wasp mimicry and diurnal habit are remarkable.
hp: Wood/stem borer. Associated with Arizona Ash.

desc: Small dark erebid whose larvae feed on sumac (Rhus). Very common at lights in chaparral. Often one of the first moths at blacklight sheets.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Lemonadeberry.

desc: Small white moth resembling the true yucca moth but a cheater in the yucca-moth mutualism. Unlike Tegeticula, does not pollinate — larvae feed in yucca fruit without providing the pollination service. A parasite of the mutualism.
hp: Obligate mutualist with Yucca whipplei. Only pollinator of yucca flowers. On Yucca (yucca).

desc: Tiny variable noctuid — one of the most abundant small moths at lights. Green, brown, or gray forms. Larvae on grasses.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. On Poaceae (grasses). Attracted to lights at night.

desc: Brown noctuid with woodgrain pattern. Spring species — among the first moths at lights each year. Larvae on many hardwoods.
hp: Stored product pest of grain and dried foods. Attracted to lights at night.

desc: Venerable dart — gray-brown noctuid. Larvae are cutworms. Common fall species at blacklights. Part of the diverse dart moth genus Agrotis.
hp: Larvae are cutworms.

desc: Pink-shaded fern moth — tiny moth with beautiful pink-and-olive forewing pattern. Despite small size (10mm wingspan), one of the most exquisitely marked micro-moths. Larvae on grasses. The pink-rose flush on olive-green background is distinctive and lovely.

fm.Color: Pink and olive bands — colorful for tiny moth

hp: Olive (Olea europaea) pest — larvae in olive fruit. Associated with Rose.

desc: Pink-banded noctuid — small moth with distinctive pink-salmon median band across gray forewings. On ragweed (Ambrosia) and other composites. The pink band is unusual and diagnostic — most small noctuids are uniformly gray-brown. Desert and foothill scrub.
hp: Common at lights. Associated with Western Ragweed.

desc: Climbing dart — pale whitish noctuid of desert habitats. Common at blacklights in eastern LA County desert margins. Larvae on low desert plants — a cutworm, feeding at soil surface. Named 'scandens' (climbing). Part of the large Euxoa dart moth radiation in western deserts.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Desert habitat. Family Noctuidae. Common species.

desc: Rich russet-orange noctuid with warm autumnal coloring. Fall-flying — one of the most colorful moths at October blacklights. Larvae on oak. The deep rufous-orange ground color is unusual and distinctive among the predominantly gray noctuid fauna.
hp: Larvae on Quercus agrifolia (coast live oak). Part of oak moth community. Family Noctuidae. Red-winged.

desc: Large brown noctuid with coppery-orange hindwings concealed at rest. When disturbed, flashes the copper hindwings as a startle display. Cryptic bark-pattern forewings. Common at lights and bait traps in fall. Larvae on many deciduous trees.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Attracted to lights. Family Noctuidae. Common species.

desc: Gray-brown noctuid with distinctive silver U-shaped mark (resembling the Greek letter 'ou') on forewing. Larvae (semi-loopers) pest of lettuce, beans, and brassicas. Hovers at flowers at dusk like a hummingbird. The silver U-mark is diagnostic.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Black Mustard.

desc: Granulate cutworm moth — dark brown dart with pinkish tinge. Larvae damage vegetables and cotton underground — feeding on roots and crowns at soil level. Year-round in SoCal. Part of the Agrotis cutworm complex — several species with similar gray-brown adults and damaging larvae.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Noctuidae. Pink.

desc: Small gray-brown noctuid with subtle mottled pattern. Introduced from Europe. Larvae on low plants. Very common at lights.
hp: Introduced species. Attracted to lights.

desc: Darwin's emerald — bright green geometrid with delicate white crosslines. One of several emerald geometrids in LA County — identification requires careful pattern analysis. Larvae on oaks. Named for Charles Darwin. The vivid green fades after death — specimens turn blue-white.
hp: Larvae on Quercus agrifolia (coast live oak). Part of oak moth community. Family Geometridae.

desc: Tulip-tree beauty — large gray geometrid with bark-like pattern. Among the largest geometrid moths in LA County — dramatic wingspan. Inchworm larvae on hardwoods. The large size for a geometrid makes this species notable — most geometrids are medium to small.
hp: Larvae on hardwoods.

desc: Linden looper — males tan with dark crosslines; females completely wingless (one of several geometrid species with wingless females). Late fall species — emerges after first cold snap when few other moths are flying. Larvae defoliate hardwoods in spring.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Geometridae. Dogwood.

desc: Olive-green noctuid with beautiful pink-salmon flush on forewings. Larvae feed exclusively on ferns — unusual for a noctuid (most eat flowering plants). The combination of olive-green and pink-rose coloring makes this one of the more attractive small noctuids.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Noctuidae. Fern.

desc: Peppered moth — the most famous moth in evolutionary biology. Classic textbook example of industrial melanism — dark forms became more common on soot-darkened tree bark during industrialization, reversing when air cleaned. Pale with dark speckling. Larvae on many trees.

fm.Color: White peppered with black — textbook evolution

hp: Larvae on many trees. Pepper-and-salt.

desc: Engrailed — gray geometrid with dark crosslines and bands. Very common at lights spring through fall. Highly variable wing pattern — some individuals very dark, others pale with heavy speckling. One of the most frequently encountered geometrids in LA. Larvae on many trees.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Attracted to lights. Family Geometridae. Common species. Attracted to lights at night.

desc: Common pug — tiny gray geometrid with four dark discal spots on forewings. The most common pug moth in LA. Eupithecia is a massive genus — over 1,400 species worldwide, most requiring genitalia for identification. Larvae eat flowers and developing seeds (unusual for geometrids).
hp: Geometrid moth — inchworm larvae on trees/shrubs. Bird food. Four-spotted.

desc: Dark noctuid with pale kidney-shaped reniform mark. Major worldwide pest of brassicas — larvae bore into cabbage, cauliflower, and broccoli heads, contaminating with frass. Distinguished from similar noctuids by the heart-and-dart pattern on the forewing.
hp: Wood/stem borer. Associated with Black Mustard.

desc: Pale tan with dark spots and streaks. Larvae spectacular: covered in dense tufts of white and yellow hairs forming lush tussocks on sycamore leaves. Very common along LA's sycamore-lined streams and canyons. The hairy caterpillars are more often seen than adults.
hp: On western sycamore (platanus racemosa). On Platanus (sycamore). Canyon habitat.

desc: Cream-tan with elegant checkered/tessellated pattern. Larvae spectacularly hairy — dense tufts of black and white hairs (tussocks). Common at lights summer through fall. Larvae on many deciduous trees. The checkered adult pattern is distinctive and attractive.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Ant associate. Attracted to lights at night. Densely hairy body.

desc: Gray with distinctive dark dagger-shaped stigma marks on forewings. Larvae spectacular: bright green with red-orange spots on oak. Fall-flying species. The 'dagger' marking is diagnostic of the genus — a line extending from orbicular to margin.
hp: Larvae on Quercus agrifolia (coast live oak). Part of oak moth community. On oaks (Quercus).

desc: Fringed noctuid — distinctive scalloped/fringed hindwing margins (fimbriaris = fringed). Desert and foothill noctuid. Larvae on low desert plants. The scalloped hindwing edge is unusual and diagnostic — most noctuids have smooth wing margins.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Desert habitat. Family Noctuidae. Common species.

desc: Gray noctuid with distinctive pointed, hood-like thorax. Larvae are spectacular: vividly yellow-black-and-white striped caterpillars on asters and goldenrod. The hooded thorax is diagnostic of Cucullia — no other noctuids have this profile.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Caterpillar predator.

desc: Close-lined quaker — white and gray noctuid with sharp, clean dark crosslines creating an attractive contrast pattern. Larvae on birch and alder. The clean black-on-white pattern is unusual among noctuids, which are typically more diffusely marked. Attractive.
hp: On alder (alnus) in riparian habitat

desc: Snowy crambid — small pure white moth very common at lights. Larvae feed in grass thatch. One of the most frequently seen moths at porch lights year-round.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Attracted to lights at night.

desc: Brown crambid with distinctive translucent wing patches. Very common at lights spring through fall. Larvae on grasses. One of the most frequently encountered crambids in LA — the translucent wing areas help distinguish from similar brown moths.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. On Poaceae (grasses). Family Crambidae

desc: Pale carrot moth — pale straw-colored crambid. Larvae damage carrot and parsley seed heads — boring into developing umbels. Common in herb gardens. The pale straw coloring and host association distinguish it from other similar-sized crambids.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Garden associate. In gardens and urban areas. Carrot.

desc: Tiny (12mm) brown-tan gelechiid. Larvae skeletonize leaves of many deciduous trees, sometimes in great numbers. Named for the 'palmerworm' — an agricultural pest in orchards. Very common but tiny — easily overlooked despite abundance.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Gelechiidae.

desc: Contracted datana — tan-brown prominent moth. Gregarious caterpillars on walnut and hickory perform dramatic group defense — all simultaneously arch heads and tails upward when disturbed, creating an alarming spiny display. The synchronized arching is remarkable.
hp: On walnut (juglans) in riparian/foothill habitat. Spiny/armed.

desc: Small dark-bodied moth with vivid ruby-red hindwings revealed in flight. Early-season flier — one of the first moths of spring. Woolly bear caterpillar. The red hindwings flash as a startle display when disturbed. Common and widespread.
hp: Early-spring flier — among first moths each year. Ruby-red hindwings. Woolly bear larvae on diverse low-growing plants.

desc: Pure white with small black dots. Bright orange-yellow abdomen. Similar to Virginia tiger moth. Common at lights.

fm.Color: White with black dots; orange abdomen

hp: Pure white moth with orange-yellow abdomen. Larvae feed on diverse herbaceous plants. Common at lights in suburban and agricultural areas.

desc: Pink-lined emerald — pale white geometrid with delicate pink crosslines. Larvae on willow. At lights near riparian areas. Delicate and attractive — the pink lines on white background are distinctive. Named 'erythemaria' (red-marked) for the pink crosslines.
hp: Larvae on Salix (willows) in riparian habitat. Attracted to lights at night.

desc: Pale geometrid with single sharp dark crossline from apex to inner margin. Larvae on knotweed and docks (Polygonaceae). The elegant minimalist pattern — a single clean line on pale wings — makes this one of the most aesthetically pleasing small geometrids.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Ant associate. Geometridae.
