desc: Large bright yellow geometrid shaped like a dead leaf. Deeply notched wing margins enhance the leaf disguise. Larvae on maple, oak, and other deciduous trees. Fall-flying. One of the few bright yellow geometrids — most are brown or gray.
hp: Larvae on Quercus agrifolia (coast live oak). Part of oak moth community. Larvae on maple, oak, and other deciduous trees.

desc: Small gray pyralid whose larvae bore into legume seed pods, destroying developing seeds. Important pest of lima beans, soybeans, and other legumes. Cosmopolitan — found wherever legumes are cultivated. Named for German entomologist Zincken.
hp: Wood/stem borer. Ant associate.

desc: Tiny moth with distinctive white head and thorax. Very common indoors — larvae eat plant debris, dried food scraps, wool, and fabrics. One of the most frequently encountered indoor moths. The white 'shoulders' are diagnostic.
hp: Concealer moths — larvae in leaf litter and dead plant material. Ant associate.

desc: Small furry moth. Larvae ('asps') are extremely venomous — covered with urticating hairs causing intense pain. Rare but medically important.

fm.Warning: Larvae extremely venomous — intense pain from hairs

hp: Larvae ('asps') extremely venomous — urticating spines cause intense pain. On oaks and ornamentals.

desc: Emerald geometrid — bright green geometrid moth with delicate white crosslines. One of the most beautiful small moths. Fades to pale after death. Larvae on legumes.

fm.Color: Bright emerald green with white crosslines

hp: Larvae on legumes.

desc: Small gray-brown erebid of leaf litter habitats. Larvae on dead leaves and detritus. Very common at lights but easily overlooked.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Attracted to lights. Attracted to lights at night. Pale.

desc: Small tortricid whose larvae bore into acorns, filberts, and chestnuts. Important nut pest. Bell-shaped at rest with coppery wing markings.
hp: Wood/stem borer. Ant associate.

desc: Black forewings with cream stripes; orange-red hindwings with black spots. Classic tiger moth pattern. Woolly bear caterpillars on low plants.

fm.Color: Black FW + cream stripes; orange-red HW + spots

hp: Diverse — tiger moths, underwings, tussock moths. Family Erebidae. Caterpillar predator.

desc: Tiny metallic moth with six dark bands. Day-flying. Larvae mine in mullein leaves. Beautiful under magnification.

fm.Color: Metallic with six dark bands; day-flying

hp: Day-flying leaf-roller moth. Larvae mine and fold mullein (Verbascum) leaves. Metallic iridescent.

desc: Small gray-brown noctuid with distinctive dark eye mark on forewing. Common at lights in chaparral habitat.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Chaparral habitat. In chaparral habitat. Dark-eyed.

desc: Lunate zale — large gray noctuid with scalloped wing margins and lunate (crescent) marks. Cryptic bark-pattern. Common at lights and bait traps in oak woodland.

fm.Color: Gray with scalloped margins; crescent marks

hp: On oak bark. Part of bark-dwelling arthropod community. Oak woodland habitat.

desc: Ruddy dart — gray-brown noctuid with warm reddish tinge. Fall-flying — one of the moths marking the end of the warm season. Common at blacklights in foothill areas. Part of the diverse dart moth complex — several fall-flying species in LA County.
hp: Fall-flying noctuid — marks the end of warm season. Common at blacklights in foothill chaparral. Larvae on herbaceous plants.

desc: Willow prominent — gray prominent moth with bark-like pattern. Larvae on willow. Tented wings at rest give distinctive profile.

fm.Color: Gray bark-pattern; tented wings at rest

hp: Larvae on Salix (willows) in riparian habitat. Gray prominent moth with bark-like pattern

desc: Green cutworm moth — pale green-gray noctuid. Larvae are cutworms on clover and alfalfa. Common at lights spring-fall.
hp: Alfalfa associate — larvae or adults feed on alfalfa (Medicago sativa). Attracted to lights at night.

desc: Brown underwing moth with marked FW. Common at lights in oak woodland and chaparral. Larvae on various shrubs.
hp: Larvae on Quercus agrifolia (coast live oak). Part of oak moth community. In chaparral habitat.

desc: Coral bean moth — brown crambid with intricate pattern. Larvae bore into coral bean (Erythrina) seeds and stems. Common wherever coral trees grow.
hp: Wood/stem borer. Crambidae.

desc: Slender emerald — delicate pale green geometrid with faint crosslines. One of several emerald moths in LA County. Larvae on various trees.
hp: Larvae on various trees. Pink-margined.

desc: Gray noctuid with orange-red hindwings. Flash-and-vanish display. Common in desert and foothill habitats.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Desert habitat. Desert specialist. Divergent.

desc: Irrorated geometrid — pale gray geometrid peppered with dark scales. Very common at lights. Larvae on many shrubs.

fm.Color: Pale gray peppered with dark scales

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Scale insect associate.

desc: Small brown concealer moth introduced from Australia. Larvae feed on dead eucalyptus leaves. Very common wherever eucalyptus grows.
hp: On eucalyptus — introduced with host tree. Introduced species. Half-marked.

desc: Brown crambid moth found in chaparral and woodland. Larvae web leaves of various shrubs. Common at lights.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Chaparral habitat. In chaparral habitat. Dangerous.

desc: Pale straw noctuid with dark spots. Similar to corn earworm but smaller. Larvae on phlox and other flowers.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Darker-spotted.

desc: Straight-lined seed moth — tiny noctuid with straight dark crossline on pale wings. Larvae feed on Asteraceae seed heads. Very common at lights.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Attracted to lights. Attracted to lights at night. Very small species.

desc: Brown noctuid with ochre-yellow hindwings. Flash display in flight. Common in desert and foothill habitats. Flies to lights.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Desert habitat. Desert specialist. Ochre.

desc: Tiny gelechiid whose larvae induce swollen stem galls on coyote brush (Baccharis). Very common in coastal sage scrub. Galls diagnostic.

fm.Color: Gray; stem galls on Baccharis diagnostic

hp: Gall-inducer on host plant. Associated with Coyote Brush.

desc: White tiger moth with translucent wings. Spectacular. Larvae ('woolly bears') on many low plants. Foothill and mountain species.

fm.Color: White with translucent patches; woolly bear larvae

hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Edwards'.

desc: Small crambid with distinctive dark belt across pale wings. Larvae in grass thatch. Common at lights in late summer.
hp: On grasses and forbs in grassland. Attracted to lights at night.

desc: Tiny noctuid. One of the smallest noctuids — under ½ inch wingspan. Larvae on everlasting (Gnaphalium) flower heads.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Noctuidae. Very small species. Moth. Eublemma.

desc: Royal poinciana moth — brown-gray erebid with undulating crosslines. Desert and foothill species. Larvae on legumes including mesquite.
hp: On mesquite (prosopis) in desert habitat. Desert specialist.

desc: Pure white tiger moth with black spots. Bright yellow-orange abdomen. Woolly bear caterpillars. Beautiful — one of the whitest moths.

fm.Color: Pure white with scattered black spots; orange abdomen

hp: Diverse — tiger moths, underwings, tussock moths. Family Erebidae. Caterpillar predator.

desc: Eight-barred moth — brown crambid with distinctive pattern of eight dark bands on translucent wings. Tropical species reaching SoCal. Larvae on legumes.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Eight-barred.

desc: Bizarre tiny moth whose larvae are external parasites of planthoppers. Larvae feed on planthopper hemolymph. Extremely unusual biology for Lepidoptera.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Parasitoid. Very small species.

desc: Small brown tortricid. Larvae bore into cotton bolls, okra pods, and hollyhock buds. Common garden pest. Native to South America.
hp: Wood/stem borer. Garden associate.

desc: Clearwing moth with transparent wings mimicking a small wasp. Larvae bore in buckwheat (Eriogonum) roots. Common in sage scrub. Day-flying.
hp: Family Sesiidae. Associated with California Buckwheat.

desc: Tiny plume moth with deeply divided feathery wings. Larvae bore in lantana flower heads. Introduced for biocontrol of invasive lantana.

fm.Color: Tan; feathery T-shaped wings; on lantana

hp: Introduced for biological pest control. Deliberately introduced for biological pest control. Tiny. Larvae bore in lantana flower heads.

desc: Green noctuid with intricate dark markings. Named for early emergence in late winter/early spring. Larvae on conifers. One of the earliest-flying moths.

fm.Season: Late winter — one of earliest moths

hp: Larvae on conifers.

desc: Pale geometrid of chaparral and scrub. Larvae on buckwheat and other native shrubs. Common at lights in foothill areas.
hp: Larvae on Eriogonum (buckwheat). Sage scrub moth. In chaparral habitat.

desc: Pale tan tiger moth with faint pattern. Spectacular hairy caterpillars. Common in foothill and mountain oak woodland.

fm.Color: Pale tan with faint darker spots

hp: Larvae on Quercus agrifolia (coast live oak). Part of oak moth community. Oak woodland habitat.

desc: Small pyralid similar to raisin moth. Larvae in dried fruit. Important stored-product pest in California's raisin and fig industry.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Ant associate. Pyralidae.

desc: Brown-gray erebid with complex line pattern. Common in desert and foothill habitats. Larvae on mesquite and acacia.
hp: On mesquite (prosopis) in desert habitat. Desert specialist.

desc: Western poplar clearwing — clearwing moth with transparent wings mimicking a wasp. Larvae bore in poplar, willow, and cottonwood trunks. Day-flying.

fm.Behavior: Day-flying; on poplar/willow

hp: Larvae on Salix (willows) in riparian habitat. Mimicry specialist.

desc: Small crambid with subtle metallic sheen on wings. Larvae in stored products and detritus. Common at lights.
hp: Stored product pest of dried foods and grain. Attracted to lights at night.

desc: Small pyralid whose larvae bore into sunflower seed heads. Can damage commercial sunflower crops. Common in LA wherever sunflowers grow.
hp: On sunflowers/composites (asteraceae).

desc: Tiny plume moth with deeply divided feathery wings. Larvae bore into geranium flower buds. Common wherever geraniums are grown.
hp: Wood/stem borer. Pterophoridae.

desc: Medium moth with spotted wings. Larvae on manzanita (Arctostaphylos). Common in chaparral. Attractive black-and-white wing pattern.
hp: On Arctostaphylos (manzanita) in chaparral. Native shrub specialist. In chaparral habitat.

desc: Gray-brown erebid. Common at lights in oak woodland. Cryptic bark-pattern when resting on tree trunks.
hp: On oak bark. Part of bark-dwelling arthropod community. Oak woodland habitat.

desc: Variable green-brown geometrid flying in winter. One of the few moths active in cold season. Larvae on oak.
hp: Larvae on Quercus agrifolia (coast live oak). Part of oak moth community. Family Geometridae. Oak Winter.

desc: Brown-gray noctuid. Larvae feed on elderberry (Sambucus). Common at lights near riparian areas. Cryptic at rest.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Associated with Elderberry.

desc: Small brown moth. Larvae feed on dead plant material, paper, and dried foods. Found in stored products and greenhouses. Pantropical.
hp: Stored product pest of dried foods and grain. Ant associate.

desc: Pale graphic moth — gray moth with pale hindwings. Flash-and-vanish display — reveals hindwings in flight. Desert and foothill habitat.

fm.Color: Gray FW; pale HW in flight

hp: Arid scrub habitat.

desc: Pyralid moth with distinctive dark crosslines. Larvae on various plants. Common at lights in foothill habitats. Part of the diverse pyralid fauna.

desc: Pale spring-flying noctuid with somewhat translucent, glassy wing texture. At sugar baits and lights. Named for the unusual wing transparency. One of several spring-emerging Orthosia quaker moths — important early-season pollinators of willows.
hp: Willow; pollinator. Associated with Red Willow.

desc: Morrison's pero — large gray-brown geometrid named for H.K. Morrison, 19th-century lepidopterist. Larvae on various trees. At lights. Distinguished from other Pero by subtle wing pattern elements. Large for a geometrid — impressive wingspan among inchworm moths.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Attracted to lights. Family Geometridae. Rare. Morrison's.

desc: Colorful noctuid with golden-orange and dark markings. On composites in desert scrub. Day-flying. Attractive.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Desert habitat. Family Noctuidae. Desert specialist. Brightly colored.

desc: Similar to T. californiata. Hibernates in caves and cool sheltered spots. Papery/tissue-like wing texture.
hp: Cave-hibernating geometrid — shelters in caves and cool crevices in winter. Papery tissue-like wing texture. Larvae on Rhamnus (coffeeberry).

desc: Sweet clover root borer — tiny cosmopterigid whose larvae bore into sweet clover roots. Potential biocontrol of invasive sweet clover. Named for entomologist B.D. Walsh.
hp: On clover (Trifolium) and related legumes (Fabaceae). Invasive.

desc: Green-gold noctuid. Larvae are semi-loopers (move with a looping gait despite having more prolegs than true loopers) on beans and various crops. At lights. Distinguished from the more common soybean looper by wing pattern details.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Attracted to lights. Family Noctuidae. Attracted to lights at night. Lesser.

desc: Small noctuid resting inside flower heads matching host flower color. Larvae in flower heads.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Noctuidae. Buta.

desc: Small flower-matching noctuid. Rests inside flowers by day. Larvae develop inside flower heads.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Ligea's.

desc: Mountain mahogany looper — gray geometrid whose inchworm larvae defoliate mountain mahogany (Cercocarpus). Important in chaparral ecology.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Chaparral habitat. Family Geometridae. In chaparral habitat. Mountain.

desc: Dark pyralid — small gray-brown pyralid. Larvae feed on oaks, spinning silk among leaves. At lights. Named 'caliginella' (dark/dusky). Part of the large Acrobasis radiation on oaks — several species in LA, all similar and requiring careful examination for identification.
hp: Larvae on Quercus agrifolia (coast live oak). Part of oak moth community. Larvae feed on oaks, spinning silk among leaves.

desc: Small gray-green noctuid with subtle olive-green wash on forewings. Larvae on various herbaceous plants. Uncommon — less frequently collected than many noctuids. The greenish tinge is unusual and distinguishes it from the gray majority of noctuids.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Green-tinged.

desc: Small pyralid of chaparral and foothill habitats. At lights. Named for wing pattern resembling a greeting gesture. On various plants.
hp: Stored product pests, wax moths, snout moths. Diverse larval habits.

desc: Tiny moth whose larvae tie oak leaves together with silk. On various oaks. Part of the diverse oak-feeding moth community.

fm.Color: Brown; ties oak leaves with silk

hp: Larvae feed on oak foliage in woodland. Associated with Coast Live Oak. On oaks (Quercus).

desc: Desert footman — tiny lichen moth of desert habitats. Larvae eat lichens growing on desert rocks and tree bark — one of the few moth groups feeding on lichens. Delicate and small. Part of the diverse tribe Lithosiini — lichen moths, all feeding on cryptogamic organisms.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Desert habitat. Desert specialist. Very small species.

desc: Small pyralid whose larvae eat scale insects. Important natural enemy. One of few Lepidoptera that are predatory.

fm.Note: Predatory larvae eat scale insects — unusual for moths

hp: Predator of scale insects — biocontrol in orchards and gardens. Preys on scale insect. Predatory.

desc: Medium geometrid with sickle-shaped (falcate) wing tips. Desert and foothill habitats. At lights.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Desert habitat. Desert specialist. Falcate.

desc: Tiny spring-flying noctuid. Day-active in wildflower meadows. Larvae on native wildflowers. Uncommon.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Noctuidae. Very small species. Moth. Annaphila.

desc: Tiny pale tineoid moth. Larvae on dead plant material. Part of the detritivore micro-moth guild.
hp: Detritivore — breaks down organic matter. Ant associate.

desc: Hutson's bird-dropping moth — tiny noctuid resembling a bird dropping when at rest — brown-and-white splotchy pattern convincingly mimics excrement. On composites (Asteraceae). At lights. The camouflage strategy is remarkably effective against visually hunting predators.
hp: Bird dropping mimic — cryptic camouflage on leaf surfaces.

desc: Small pale moth with upturned wing tips. On various trees. Part of the diverse western micro-moth fauna.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Ypsolophidae.

desc: Small noctuid with warm reddish coloring unusual among typically gray-brown noctuids. Uncommon in collections. Desert and foothill habitats. The red tinge makes this one of the more distinctively colored small noctuids. Named for entomologist Slosson.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Desert habitat. Family Noctuidae. Rare. Red-tinged.

desc: Small brown crambid associated with prickly pear cactus. Larvae feed in cactus detritus and under pads.
hp: Specialist on cactus (cactus spp.). On Opuntia (cactus). Crambidae.

desc: Tiny tortricid with diagnostic contrasting dark and pale wing pattern. On various trees including willows and oaks. At lights. Part of the enormous genus Epinotia — over 200 NA species, most tiny and requiring genitalia for identification.
hp: Oak; willow. Associated with Coast Live Oak and Red Willow. On Quercus (oak).

desc: Gray moth named for J.G.O. Tepper, early California entomologist. On composites in desert scrub. At lights. Part of the small genus Plagiomimicus — a primarily southwestern group associated with Asteraceae in arid habitats.
hp: Mimicry — Batesian or aggressive. Desert habitat.

desc: Small gray noctuid named 'tertia' (third) as the third species described in the genus. At lights in foothill and mountain habitats. Part of the diverse western noctuid fauna — many such small gray moths require specimen dissection for firm identification.
hp: Larvae likely on native shrubs.

desc: Pallid wave moth — small pale geometrid of desert and foothill habitats. At lights. Named for its pallid (washed-out pale) coloring. Part of the large western Digrammia radiation — a genus of subtle pale geometrids, many requiring genitalia for definitive identification.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Desert habitat. Desert specialist. Large species. Geometridae.

desc: Extremely primitive moth (Eriocraniidae) with metallic gold-blue scales. Mines oak leaves. Among the most ancient moth lineages still living.
hp: On coast live oak (quercus agrifolia). On Quercus (oak). Metallic coloring.

desc: Implicated carpet moth — medium geometrid of woodland habitats. Larvae on various herbaceous plants. At lights. Named 'implicata' (implicated/involved). Part of the large family Geometridae — 'earth-measurers,' named for the looping (inchworm) gait of the larvae.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Attracted to lights. Family Geometridae. Attracted to lights at night. Large species.

desc: Small golden-yellow noctuid with dark marks. Named for collector B. Neumoegen. On composites in chaparral.
hp: On composites in chaparral.

desc: Tiny tortricid with diagnostic white patch at wing angle. On various trees including oaks and willows. At lights. Part of the enormous Tortricidae — the third-largest moth family, with 10,000+ species worldwide. Larvae in buds, shoots, or leaves.
hp: Oak; willow. Associated with Coast Live Oak and Red Willow. On Quercus (oak).

desc: Medium carpenter moth. Larvae bore in tree trunks and roots — can take 2-3 years to develop. Thick-bodied. At lights.
hp: Wood-boring detritivore.

desc: California dejongia — tiny oecophorid moth. Part of California's poorly known micro-moth fauna — thousands of species in families like Oecophoridae, Gelechiidae, and Tineidae remain understudied. On native plants. The California micro-moth fauna is still being described.
hp: Concealer moths — larvae in leaf litter and dead plant material. Oecophoridae.

desc: Large lasiocampid (lappet moth). Larvae with dense hair tufts. On oaks and other trees. Named for Medusa's snake-like hair.
hp: Larvae on Quercus agrifolia (coast live oak). Part of oak moth community. Larvae with dense hair tufts.

desc: Extremely primitive moth from ancient lineage (Acanthopteroctetidae). Tiny. Metallic. California endemic family. Among the most primitive Lepidoptera.

fm.Note: Ancient lineage — among most primitive moths

hp: Extremely primitive moth from ancient lineage. Leaf miner. Living fossil of moth evolution.

desc: Tiny leaf-mining moth. Larvae make blotch mines in leaves. Metallic coloring visible only under magnification.
hp: Leaf miner — blotch mines in various leaves. Metallic wing scales. Tiny but beautiful.

desc: Small gray noctuid of the diverse western Sympistis radiation. Named for entomologist M. Ragan. At lights. Part of a genus containing over 100 western NA species — identification often requires genitalia examination. Mountain and foothill habitats.
hp: Part of the speciose western Sympistis radiation. At lights in montane habitat. Larvae on native plants. Named for entomologist M.

desc: Another extremely primitive eriocraniid moth. Leaf miner on oaks. Ancient lineage predating most other Lepidoptera families.
hp: Leaf miner on oak — larvae create serpentine or blotch mines inside leaves.

desc: Tiny moth whose larvae mine encelia (brittlebush) leaves in coastal sage scrub. Host-specific.
hp: Leaf miner on sage scrub plants. Associated with Black Sage and Bush Sunflower.

desc: Spectacular tiny day-flying heliozelid with metallic scales. Mines four o'clock (Mirabilis) leaves. Among the most beautiful micro-moths.

fm.Color: Metallic jewel-like; day-flying; on four o'clock plants

hp: Day-flying heliozelid. Metallic leaf miner on Mirabilis (four o'clock). Jewel-like iridescence.

desc: Tiny dark tortricid on native trees and shrubs. Named 'truncatella' for the truncated (squared-off) wing tip. Part of the vast Tortricidae diversity — California harbors hundreds of tortricid species, most still poorly known biologically.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Tortricidae.

desc: Tiny noctuid of early spring. Day-flying among wildflowers. Part of the specialized spring-flying noctuid guild.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Noctuidae. Very small species. Moth. Annaphila.

desc: Tiny pale crambid with single diagnostic dark forewing spot. On grasses. Larvae in grass stems or thatch. At lights. Distinguished from other tiny pale crambids by the single spot — most have multiple markings or crosslines.
hp: Acoustic signaler — song/stridulation important in mate location. Prey for bats and birds. On Poaceae (grasses).

desc: Small gray moth with six distinctive dark spots arranged in two rows on forewings. On composites (Asteraceae) in coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Spring flier. Part of a small Neotropical genus — this species reaches its northern range limit in SoCal.
hp: Coastal sage scrub and chaparral. Associated with Black Sage. On Asteraceae (composites).

desc: Elsinore white — small white geometrid with subtle markings. Named for the Elsinore area (Lake Elsinore). One of several white geometrids in LA — distinguished by subtle pattern elements. At lights. Larvae on various trees.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Attracted to lights. Family Geometridae. Attracted to lights at night. Elsinore.

desc: Small gray pyralid named for collector William Field. At lights in chaparral and foothill habitats. Larvae on various plants.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Chaparral habitat. In chaparral habitat. Field's.

desc: Five-spotted twirler — tiny gelechiid with five dark spots on forewings. Larvae feed inside plant stems or seed heads. At lights. 'Twirler moths' (Gelechiidae) are named for the habit of twirling or spinning rapidly when disturbed.
hp: Part of local invertebrate food web. Ant associate. Attracted to lights at night. Very small species.

desc: Small tortricid boring in snakeweed (Gutierrezia) stems. Host-specific. Foothill and desert scrub.
hp: Wood/stem borer. Desert habitat.

desc: Medium saturnid. California native. Larvae on ceanothus. One of the smaller silkmoths. Named for its discoverers.
hp: On ceanothus (california lilac) — native shrub specialist. Saturniidae.

desc: Small pale geometrid with dark crosslines. Named for French entomologist A. Guenée. At lights.
hp: Geometrid moth — inchworm larvae on trees/shrubs. Bird food. Small species. Attracted to lights. Attracted to lights at night.

desc: Small sesiid with transparent wings mimicking a wasp. Larvae bore in shrub stems. Day-flying wasp mimic.
hp: Wasp mimic — Batesian mimicry with narrow waist and warning colors.
